title: Human Development Index

infobox.value: Developing countries: [Arab States](/wiki/Arab_States "Arab States")  [East Asia](/wiki/East_Asia "East Asia") and the Pacific  [Europe](/wiki/Europe "Europe") and [Central Asia](/wiki/Central_Asia "Central Asia")  [Latin America](/wiki/Latin_America "Latin America") and the [Caribbean](/wiki/Caribbean "Caribbean")  [South Asia](/wiki/South_Asia "South Asia")  [Sub-Saharan Africa](/wiki/Sub-Saharan_Africa "Sub-Saharan Africa")

infobox.key: [OECD](/wiki/OECD "OECD")  [Europe](/wiki/Europe "Europe") (not in the OECD), and [CIS](/wiki/Commonwealth_of_Independent_States "Commonwealth of Independent States")  [Latin America](/wiki/Latin_America "Latin America") and the [Caribbean](/wiki/Caribbean "Caribbean")  [East Asia](/wiki/East_Asia "East Asia")
infobox.value: [Arab League](/wiki/Arab_League "Arab League")  [South Asia](/wiki/South_Asia "South Asia")  [Sub-Saharan Africa](/wiki/Sub-Saharan_Africa "Sub-Saharan Africa")

(**HDI**) is a statistical composite index of [life expectancy](/wiki/Life_expectancy "Life expectancy"), [education](/wiki/Education_Index "Education Index") (mean years of schooling completed and expected years of schooling upon entering the [education system](/wiki/Education_system "Education system")), and [per capita income](/wiki/Per_capita_income "Per capita income") indicators, which is used to rank countries into four tiers of [human development](/wiki/Human_development_(humanity) "Human development (humanity)"). A country scores a higher level of HDI when the [lifespan](/wiki/Life_expectancy_at_birth "Life expectancy at birth") is higher, the [education](/wiki/Education "Education") level is higher, and the gross national income [GNI (PPP) per capita](/wiki/GNI_(PPP)_per_capita "GNI (PPP) per capita") is higher. It was developed by Pakistani economist [Mahbub ul-Haq](/wiki/Mahbub_ul-Haq "Mahbub ul-Haq") and was further used to measure a country's development by the [United Nations Development Programme](/wiki/United_Nations_Development_Programme "United Nations Development Programme") (UNDP)'s Human Development Report Office.[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2)[[3]](#cite_note-auto-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4)

Composite statistic of life expectancy, education, and income indices

The **Human Development Index** (**HDI**) is a statistical composite index of [life expectancy](/wiki/Life_expectancy "Life expectancy"), [education](/wiki/Education_Index "Education Index") (mean years of schooling completed and expected years of schooling upon entering the [education system](/wiki/Education_system "Education system")), and [per capita income](/wiki/Per_capita_income "Per capita income") indicators, which is used to rank countries into four tiers of [human development](/wiki/Human_development_(humanity) "Human development (humanity)"). A country scores a higher level of HDI when the [lifespan](/wiki/Life_expectancy_at_birth "Life expectancy at birth") is higher, the [education](/wiki/Education "Education") level is higher, and the gross national income [GNI (PPP) per capita](/wiki/GNI_(PPP)_per_capita "GNI (PPP) per capita") is higher. It was developed by Pakistani economist [Mahbub ul-Haq](/wiki/Mahbub_ul-Haq "Mahbub ul-Haq") and was further used to measure a country's development by the [United Nations Development Programme](/wiki/United_Nations_Development_Programme "United Nations Development Programme") (UNDP)'s Human Development Report Office.[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2)[[3]](#cite_note-auto-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4)

The 2010 [Human Development Report](/wiki/Human_Development_Report "Human Development Report") introduced an [inequality-adjusted Human Development Index](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_inequality-adjusted_Human_Development_Index "List of countries by inequality-adjusted Human Development Index") (IHDI). While the simple HDI remains useful, it stated that "the IHDI is the actual level of human development (accounting for this [inequality](/wiki/Social_inequality "Social inequality")), while the HDI can be viewed as an index of 'potential' human development (or the maximum level of HDI) that could be achieved if there were no inequality."[[5]](#cite_note-5)

The index is based on the human development approach, developed by Mahbub ul-Haq, anchored in [Amartya Sen](/wiki/Amartya_Sen "Amartya Sen")'s work on human capabilities, and often framed in terms of whether people are able to "be" and "do" desirable things in life. Examples include — being: well-fed, sheltered, and healthy; doing: work, education, voting, participating in community life. The freedom of choice is considered central — someone choosing to be hungry (e.g. when [fasting for religious reasons](/wiki/Fasting_in_religion "Fasting in religion")) is considered different from someone who is hungry because they cannot afford to buy food, or because the country is going through a [famine](/wiki/Famine "Famine").[[6]](#cite_note-auto1-6)

The index does not take into account several factors, such as the [net wealth per capita](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_wealth_per_adult "List of countries by wealth per adult") or the relative [quality of goods](/wiki/Quality_(business) "Quality (business)") in a country. This situation tends to lower the ranking of some of the most [developed countries](/wiki/Developed_countries "Developed countries"), such as the [G7](/wiki/G7 "G7") members and others.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

## Origins

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Human_Development_Index&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Origins")]

The origins of the HDI are found in the annual Human Development Reports produced by the Human Development Report Office of the [United Nations Development Programme](/wiki/United_Nations_Development_Programme "United Nations Development Programme") (UNDP). These annual reports were devised and launched by Pakistani economist [Mahbub ul-Haq](/wiki/Mahbub_ul-Haq "Mahbub ul-Haq") in 1990, and had the explicit purpose "to shift the focus of development economics from [national income](/wiki/National_income "National income") accounting to [people-centered policies](/wiki/People-centered_development "People-centered development")". He believed that a simple composite measure of human development was needed to convince the public, academics and politicians that they can, and should, evaluate development not only by economic advances but also improvements in human [well-being](/wiki/Well-being "Well-being").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c5/Human_Development_Index_Underlying_Principles.svg/960px-Human_Development_Index_Underlying_Principles.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Human_Development_Index_Underlying_Principles.svg)

The underlying principle behind the Human Development Index[[6]](#cite_note-auto1-6)

## Dimensions and calculation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Human_Development_Index&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Dimensions and calculation")]

### New method (2010 HDI onwards)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Human_Development_Index&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: New method (2010 HDI onwards)")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c9/Human_Development_Index_regions_evolution_1990-2021-fr.svg/500px-Human_Development_Index_regions_evolution_1990-2021-fr.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Human_Development_Index_regions_evolution_1990-2021-fr.svg)

HDI trends between 1990 and 2021

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| [World](/wiki/List_of_sovereign_states "List of sovereign states")  [OECD](/wiki/OECD "OECD") countries | Developing countries: [Arab States](/wiki/Arab_States "Arab States")  [East Asia](/wiki/East_Asia "East Asia") and the Pacific  [Europe](/wiki/Europe "Europe") and [Central Asia](/wiki/Central_Asia "Central Asia")  [Latin America](/wiki/Latin_America "Latin America") and the [Caribbean](/wiki/Caribbean "Caribbean")  [South Asia](/wiki/South_Asia "South Asia")  [Sub-Saharan Africa](/wiki/Sub-Saharan_Africa "Sub-Saharan Africa") |

Published on 4 November 2010 (and updated on 10 June 2011), the 2010 Human Development Report calculated the HDI combining three dimensions:[[8]](#cite_note-8)[[9]](#cite_note-9)

* A long and healthy life: [Life expectancy at birth](/wiki/Life_expectancy_at_birth "Life expectancy at birth")
* [Education](/wiki/Education_index "Education index"): Mean years of schooling and expected years of schooling
* A decent standard of living: [GNI](/wiki/Gross_national_income "Gross national income") per capita ([PPP](/wiki/Purchasing_power_parity "Purchasing power parity") [international dollars](/wiki/International_dollar "International dollar"))

In its 2010 Human Development Report, the UNDP began using a new method of calculating the HDI. The following three indices are used:

1. Life Expectancy Index (LEI) ![{\displaystyle ={\frac {{\textrm {LE}}-20}{85-20}}={\frac {{\textrm {LE}}-20}{65}}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/ca43fe97dc2c82e390ab46975157810ba5824681)

:   :   LEI is equal to 1 when [life expectancy at birth](/wiki/Life_expectancy_at_birth "Life expectancy at birth") is 85 years, and 0 when life expectancy at birth is 20 years.

2. [Education Index](/wiki/Education_Index "Education Index") (EI) ![{\displaystyle ={\frac {{\textrm {MYSI}}+{\textrm {EYSI}}}{2}}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/322a300516420de9fa83acbd25f56e3f704e7ef8)[[10]](#cite_note-10)

:   2.1 Mean Years of Schooling Index (MYSI) ![{\displaystyle ={\frac {\textrm {MYS}}{15}}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/55aabf77d89eb9e57d504c519802826d7a7da917)[[11]](#cite_note-11)

    :   Fifteen is the projected maximum of this indicator for 2025.
:   2.2 Expected Years of Schooling Index (EYSI) ![{\displaystyle ={\frac {\textrm {EYS}}{18}}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/d144416b4a2047923a6c231941cd3099f90fd5dd)[[12]](#cite_note-12)

    :   Eighteen is equivalent to achieving a [master's degree](/wiki/Master%27s_degree "Master's degree") in most countries.

3. Income Index (II) ![{\displaystyle ={\frac {\ln({\textrm {GNIpc}})-\ln(100)}{\ln(75,000)-\ln(100)}}={\frac {\ln({\textrm {GNIpc}})-\ln(100)}{\ln(750)}}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/5a3d56371f35540c2da3bae3250266ba3508160e)

:   :   II is 1 when GNI per capita is $75,000 and 0 when GNI per capita is $100.

Finally, the HDI is the [geometric mean](/wiki/Geometric_mean "Geometric mean") of the previous three normalized indices:

:   ![{\displaystyle {\textrm {HDI}}={\sqrt[{3}]{{\textrm {LEI}}\cdot {\textrm {EI}}\cdot {\textrm {II}}}}.}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/364ecd910bf260a13250cde6a9d25e08d9633fe8)

LE: [Life expectancy at birth](/wiki/Life_expectancy_at_birth "Life expectancy at birth")  
MYS: Mean years of schooling (i.e. years that a person aged 25 or older has spent in formal education)  
EYS: Expected years of schooling (i.e. total expected years of schooling for children under 18 years of age, incl. young men and women aged 13–17)  
GNIpc: [Gross national income at purchasing power parity per capita](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_GNI_(PPP)_per_capita "List of countries by GNI (PPP) per capita")

### Old method (HDI before 2010)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Human_Development_Index&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Old method (HDI before 2010)")]

The HDI combined three dimensions last used in its 2009 report:

* [Life expectancy](/wiki/Life_expectancy "Life expectancy") at birth, as an index of population health and longevity to HDI
* Knowledge and education, as measured by the adult [literacy](/wiki/Literacy "Literacy") rate (with two-thirds weighting) and the combined primary, secondary, and tertiary [gross enrollment ratio](/wiki/Gross_enrollment_ratio "Gross enrollment ratio") (with one-third weighting).
* [Standard of living](/wiki/Standard_of_living "Standard of living"), as indicated by the [natural logarithm](/wiki/Natural_logarithm "Natural logarithm") of [gross domestic product](/wiki/Gross_domestic_product "Gross domestic product") [per capita](/wiki/Per_capita "Per capita") at [purchasing power parity](/wiki/Purchasing_power_parity "Purchasing power parity").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d7/Human_Development_Index_trends.svg/330px-Human_Development_Index_trends.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Human_Development_Index_trends.svg)

HDI trends between 1975 and 2004

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| [OECD](/wiki/OECD "OECD")  [Europe](/wiki/Europe "Europe") (not in the OECD), and [CIS](/wiki/Commonwealth_of_Independent_States "Commonwealth of Independent States")  [Latin America](/wiki/Latin_America "Latin America") and the [Caribbean](/wiki/Caribbean "Caribbean")  [East Asia](/wiki/East_Asia "East Asia") | [Arab League](/wiki/Arab_League "Arab League")  [South Asia](/wiki/South_Asia "South Asia")  [Sub-Saharan Africa](/wiki/Sub-Saharan_Africa "Sub-Saharan Africa") |

This methodology was used by the UNDP until their 2011 report.

The formula defining the HDI is promulgated by the United Nations Development Programme ([UNDP](/wiki/UNDP "UNDP")).[[13]](#cite_note-13) In general, to transform a raw [variable](/wiki/Variable_(mathematics) "Variable (mathematics)"), say ![{\displaystyle x}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/87f9e315fd7e2ba406057a97300593c4802b53e4), into a unit-free [index](/wiki/Index_(economics) "Index (economics)") between 0 and 1 (which allows different indices to be added together), the following [formula](/wiki/Formula "Formula") is used:

* ![{\displaystyle x{\text{ index}}={\frac {x-a}{b-a}}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/649cc3be2e96c9683d44387a699f36a4269c22a5)

where ![{\displaystyle a}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/ffd2487510aa438433a2579450ab2b3d557e5edc) and ![{\displaystyle b}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/f11423fbb2e967f986e36804a8ae4271734917c3) are the [lowest and highest values](/wiki/Maxima_and_minima "Maxima and minima") the variable ![{\displaystyle x}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/87f9e315fd7e2ba406057a97300593c4802b53e4) can attain, respectively.

The Human Development Index (HDI) then represents the uniformly weighted sum with 1⁄3 contributed by each of the following factor indices:

* [Life Expectancy Index](/wiki/Life_Expectancy_Index "Life Expectancy Index") ![{\displaystyle ={\frac {{\text{LE}}-25}{85-25}}={\frac {{\text{LE}}-25}{60}}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/81d8ec3412ecdeed6904a350d921510294ff313b)
* [Education Index](/wiki/Education_Index "Education Index") ![{\displaystyle ={\frac {2}{3}}\times {\text{ALI}}+{\frac {1}{3}}\times {\text{GEI}}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/8750ed50390c52ecf56ce02ad9cc822de10a60c7)
  + [Adult Literacy Index](/wiki/Adult_Literacy_Index "Adult Literacy Index") (ALI) ![{\displaystyle ={\frac {{\text{ALR}}-0}{100-0}}={\frac {\text{ALR}}{100}}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/d8a0cb2646b17ee3339af6764c8033b795109a8c)
  + [Gross Enrollment Index](/wiki/Gross_Enrollment_Index "Gross Enrollment Index") (GEI) ![{\displaystyle ={\frac {{\text{CGER}}-0}{100-0}}={\frac {\text{CGER}}{100}}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/e2cd0090ede61e15fdc081be3d1bad29f15bb2fd)
* [GDP](/wiki/GDP "GDP") ![{\displaystyle ={\frac {\log({\text{GDPpc}})-\log(100)}{\log(40000)-\log(100)}}={\frac {\log({\text{GDPpc}})-\log(100)}{\log(400)}}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/ab463d91596347b53d444a05383a38fb810bda79)

## Criticism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Human_Development_Index&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Criticism")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/12/Per-capita-co-emissions-vs-human-development-index.svg/250px-Per-capita-co-emissions-vs-human-development-index.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Per-capita-co-emissions-vs-human-development-index.svg)

HDI in relation to consumption-based CO2 emissions per capita

The Human Development Index has been criticized on a number of grounds, including focusing exclusively on national performance and ranking, lack of attention to development from a global perspective, measurement error of the underlying statistics, and on the UNDP's changes in formula which can lead to severe misclassification of "low", "medium", "high" or "very high" human development countries.[[18]](#cite_note-Wolff_et_al._2011-18)

There have also been various criticism towards the lack of consideration regarding sustainability[[19]](#cite_note-19) (later addressed by the [planetary pressures-adjusted HDI](/wiki/Planetary_pressures-adjusted_HDI "Planetary pressures-adjusted HDI")), social inequality[[20]](#cite_note-20) (addressed by the [inequality-adjusted HDI](/wiki/Inequality-adjusted_HDI "Inequality-adjusted HDI")), [unemployment](/wiki/Unemployment "Unemployment")[[21]](#cite_note-b208-21) or [democracy](/wiki/Democracy "Democracy").[[21]](#cite_note-b208-21)

The removal of [literacy](/wiki/Literacy "Literacy") from HDI has been criticized because [educational attainment](/wiki/Educational_attainment "Educational attainment") evaluates only the quantity of education but not the quality or the outcomes of education and can result in [perverse incentives](/wiki/Perverse_incentive "Perverse incentive").[[22]](#cite_note-22)

Economists Hendrik Wolff, Howard Chong and [Maximilian Auffhammer](/wiki/Maximilian_Auffhammer "Maximilian Auffhammer") discuss the HDI from the perspective of data error in the underlying health, education and income statistics used to construct the HDI. They have identified three sources of data error which are: (i) data updating, (ii) formula revisions and (iii) thresholds to classify a country's development status. They conclude that 11%, 21% and 34% of all countries can be interpreted as currently misclassified in the development bins due to the three sources of data error, respectively. Wolff, Chong and Auffhammer suggest that the United Nations should discontinue the practice of classifying countries into development bins because the cut-off values seem arbitrary, and the classifications can provide incentives for strategic behavior in reporting official statistics, as well as having the potential to misguide politicians, investors, charity donors and the public who use the HDI at large.[[18]](#cite_note-Wolff_et_al._2011-18)

In 2010, the UNDP reacted to the criticism by updating the thresholds to classify nations as low, medium, and high human development countries. In a comment to *[The Economist](/wiki/The_Economist "The Economist")* in early January 2011, the Human Development Report Office responded[[23]](#cite_note-23) to an article published in the magazine on 6 January 2011[[24]](#cite_note-24) which discusses the Wolff *et al.* paper. The Human Development Report Office states that they undertook a systematic revision of the methods used for the calculation of the HDI, and that the new methodology directly addresses the critique by Wolff *et al.* in that it generates a system for continuously updating the human-development categories whenever formula or data revisions take place.

In 2013, Salvatore Monni and Alessandro Spaventa emphasized that in the debate of GDP versus HDI, it is often forgotten that these are both external indicators that prioritize different benchmarks upon which the quantification of societal welfare can be predicated. The larger question is whether it is possible to shift the focus of policy from a battle between competing paradigms to a mechanism for eliciting information on well-being directly from the population.[[25]](#cite_note-Monni_and_Spaventa,_2013-25)
